title: Measures of Anchoring in Estimation Tasks
delta_note: Global equivalence margin (standardized units). Cohen (1988) defines d < 0.2 as 'negligible' effect.

evaluation_framework.description: Two-tier evaluation: phenomenon-level and data-level matching

type_1.name: phenomenon_level_match
type_1.description: Tests whether agent exhibits anchoring effect
type_1.approach: Independent t-tests comparing high vs low anchor conditions

type_2.name: data_level_match
type_2.description: Tests how closely agent data matches human baseline data using equivalence testing
type_2.approach: TOST (Two One-Sided Tests) for anchoring index and effect sizes

required_tests.description: Tests whether agent shows anchoring for Washington presidency question
Tests whether agent shows anchoring for Chicago population question
Tests whether agent shows anchoring for Mt. Everest boiling point question
TOST equivalence test: agent anchoring index vs human baseline

method.rationale: Tests if overall anchoring strength matches human baseline

required_tests.description: TOST equivalence test: agent effect sizes vs human baseline

method.rationale: Tests if effect size magnitude matches human baseline

scoring.critical_requirement: All phenomenon-level tests (P1, P2, P3) must pass

key_findings.main_finding: People's numerical estimates are systematically biased toward arbitrary anchor values, even when they know the anchors are random and uninformative
key_findings.theoretical_implication: Demonstrates insufficient adjustment from anchors in numerical estimation tasks
key_findings.practical_implication: Anchoring effects can influence judgments in many domains including negotiations, pricing, and forecasting

notes.replication_notes: This is a foundational study on anchoring. The effect is very robust and replicates across cultures and contexts.
notes.implementation_notes: Present anchors as 'randomly generated' to maintain the uninformative nature. Use diverse questions to test generalizability.
notes.common_issues: Some sophisticated reasoners may deliberately counter-anchor. This is normal and expected in small numbers.
